category_summary:
The AI community buzzed with evidence that AI is finally moving the economic needle. **Erik Brynjolfsson** [presented a compelling case](/?date=2026-02-19&category=social#item-1cdf7a9312c6) in the **Financial Times** that AI is beginning to boost productivity, citing 2.7% US productivity growth in 2025—double the prior decade's average.

- **Anthropic** [published landmark research](/?date=2026-02-19&category=social#item-67875a2879db) analyzing millions of real-world agent interactions, revealing that agent autonomy is rapidly increasing—longest **Claude Code** turns [nearly doubled to 45 minutes](/?date=2026-02-19&category=social#item-0c626cbbc7df) in three months, while 73% of tool calls still maintain human oversight
- **Ethan Mollick** [released his influential AI guide](/?date=2026-02-19&category=social#item-1b5cc8d06dc2), noting the biggest changes yet as AI shifts from chatbots to full application ecosystems, and [highlighted **Anthropic's** surprising dominance](/?date=2026-02-19&category=social#item-93ec110847ac) in enterprise knowledge work tools
- **OpenAI** [introduced **EVMbench**](/?date=2026-02-19&category=social#item-dc24b4ef2687), a new benchmark measuring AI agents' ability to detect and exploit smart contract vulnerabilities, drawing 1.2M views
- **Levelsio** [went viral describing his setup](/?date=2026-02-19&category=social#item-b1fe5ec7ccaf) connecting **Claude Code** to production via Telegram, while **Matt Shumer** called **Claude Sonnet 4.6's** [near-human-level computer use](/?date=2026-02-19&category=social#item-163074735155) capabilities "extremely consequential"
- **Google** [launched **Lyria 3**](/?date=2026-02-19&category=social#item-33dc62625981), their most advanced music generation model, and a docs cleanup at **Anthropic** briefly sparked community panic about Agent SDK access before being [quickly clarified](/?date=2026-02-19&category=social#item-3e6dd74e00d4)
category_summary_html:
The AI community buzzed with evidence that AI is finally moving the economic needle. Erik Brynjolfsson presented a compelling case in the Financial Times that AI is beginning to boost productivity, citing 2.7% US productivity growth in 2025—double the prior decade's average.

  • Anthropic published landmark research analyzing millions of real-world agent interactions, revealing that agent autonomy is rapidly increasing—longest Claude Code turns nearly doubled to 45 minutes in three months, while 73% of tool calls still maintain human oversight
  • Ethan Mollick released his influential AI guide, noting the biggest changes yet as AI shifts from chatbots to full application ecosystems, and highlighted Anthropic's surprising dominance in enterprise knowledge work tools
  • OpenAI introduced EVMbench, a new benchmark measuring AI agents' ability to detect and exploit smart contract vulnerabilities, drawing 1.2M views
  • Levelsio went viral describing his setup connecting Claude Code to production via Telegram, while Matt Shumer called Claude Sonnet 4.6's near-human-level computer use capabilities "extremely consequential"
  • Google launched Lyria 3, their most advanced music generation model, and a docs cleanup at Anthropic briefly sparked community panic about Agent SDK access before being quickly clarified

themes.name: AI Productivity & Economic Impact
themes.description: Erik Brynjolfsson presents multi-source evidence that AI is beginning to boost productivity, citing 2.7% US productivity growth in 2025 (double prior decade average), micro studies, and employment data

themes.name: Anthropic Agent Autonomy Research
themes.description: Anthropic published a major empirical study analyzing millions of real-world agent interactions across Claude Code and their API, revealing patterns of increasing autonomy, human oversight behaviors, and risk levels. Key findings: 73% HITL, 0.8% irreversible actions, turn durations doubling, and experienced users shifting to auto-approval.

themes.name: Anthropic Agent SDK/MAX Policy Clarification
themes.description: Major community concern about Anthropic's docs suggesting Agent SDK usage required API keys rather than MAX subscriptions. Anthropic employee (trq212) extensively clarified this was a docs cleanup error - nothing is changing. Local dev/experimentation with MAX is encouraged; businesses building on Agent SDK should use API keys.

themes.name: AI Product Landscape & Knowledge Work
themes.description: Mollick's influential AI guide highlights the shift from chatbots to a model-app-harness framework, noting Anthropic's surprising dominance in enterprise knowledge work (PowerPoint, Excel, Cowork) while competitors focus on coding. Google's fragmented AI strategy also discussed.

themes.name: Claude Sonnet 4.6 Release & Computer Use
themes.description: Multiple posts discuss the just-released Claude Sonnet 4.6 (GA: 2026-02-17), particularly its near-human-level computer use capabilities for web navigation, spreadsheet manipulation, and multi-step form filling. Matt Shumer calls it 'extremely consequential'.

themes.name: Claude Code in Production Workflows
themes.description: Levelsio's viral thread about connecting Claude Code to Telegram for real-time production site management. Covers security incidents (social engineering of OpenClaw), VPS isolation strategies, and the vision of autonomous cron-based AI agents with SOUL.md mission files.

themes.name: Coding in Production with AI
themes.description: Levelsio's viral thread about using Claude Code to edit live production code, skipping traditional git/deploy workflows. Claims only one mistake in 12 months. Includes connecting Telegram as a mobile interface and plans to pipe error logs directly to Claude Code for autonomous fixing.

themes.name: Groq Acquisition & AI Infrastructure
themes.description: Major speculation around Groq's reported $20B acquihire and its strategic implications for AI chip/inference infrastructure.

themes.name: AI Security & Smart Contracts
themes.description: OpenAI's EVMbench benchmark for measuring AI agents' smart contract security capabilities. Very high visibility launch (1.2M views).

themes.name: Vibe Coding & AI Coding Agents
themes.description: Paul Ford's NYT op-ed on vibe coding for general audiences and Simon Willison's commentary framing post-November-2025 coding agents (like Claude Code) as fundamentally changing software development costs.

items.title: Since my op-ed in the @FT  was published on Monday (https://t.co/3gotWePS7d), there’s been a growing...
items.content:
Since my op-ed in the @FT  was published on Monday (https://t.co/3gotWePS7d), there’s been a growing debate about whether we’re beginning to see evidence that AI is boosting productivity.

First, let me be clear that the aggregate productivity data by itself is far from definitive. Even with the new revisions, there is certainly a lot of noise in US productivity numbers. No doubt lots of other factors are at work.

That said, my growing confidence that AI is powering higher productivity draws on evidence from a variety of sources:

1. The stunning capabilities of AI. If anything, I think the past decade of impressive improvements in machine learning and generative AI are still underrated. We are in the early stages of a massive economic transformation: https://t.co/HzUTZqWjNi

2. A growing number of micro studies document double-digit productivity gains in specific applications. @alexolegimas has a great catalog in his blog post: https://t.co/U1OqbpKfi4

3. My discussions with power users who use AI for coding, customer service, research and other applications, as well as more and more business executives, convince me that the facts on the ground are (finally) changing.

4. Data from our Canaries in the Coal Mine paper show employment changes in occupations most affected by AI: https://t.co/EkgB2VPyEL

5. And now, inklings in the aggregate productivity data are also telling the same story.

These are all consistent with the hypothesis that AI is beginning to have a positive impact on productivity.

The FT put a more definitive headline on my recent piece than I would have liked, but my bet (https://t.co/5m1oMbuxPj) is that we're likely to see more and more evidence as time goes on, barring some other shocks (e.g. macro mismanagement, trade wars, etc).

As each quarter goes by and we see more data, I continue to update my views.

No doubt, I'm currently out of sync with a lot of mainstream economists on this topic, but that’s ok by me.

items.title: ✨ A dream I had finally came true: I can now chat directly with my sites to build any feature or fix...
items.content:
✨ A dream I had finally came true: I can now chat directly with my sites to build any feature or fix any bug just via Telegram

I've been playing with OpenClaw for 3 weeks now and it's great but I was always too scared to run it on any production server

And I was right a bit as @marckohlbrugge was able to hack it by social engineering and acting as if it was me, and with enough tries it believed him, and was able to modify the server, change SSH keys etc. of course I had it isolated properly on its own VPS and it didn't touch anything sensitive (as it should!)

Marc then reported that bug to @steipete who patched it fast

But I wanted to try something more basic and simple, and I think maybe more secure: to just connect Claude Code on my server to Telegram which would be hard locked to only messages from me

So I installed claude-code-telegram by @RichardAtCT on the server and run it as a system daemon and it works really well

The cool thing is that I was already using Telegram for server errors like this:

> Photo AI - ❌ Random credits giveaway failed (Attempt 30/30) with an exception: SQLSTATE[HY000]: General error: 5 database is locked

So now I can just reply, "Ok fix this", and Claude Code on the server in production will try (and probably succeed) in fixing it

In the video below I asked it to make show [🌳 Parks ] on the map by default on load, it did that, then I reloaded the page and it instantly worked

One thing it still needs is sending actual messages while it's doing stuff which OpenClaw does really well, it's annoying to just wait while it says "Working..." but that's probably next
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Following [Reddit](/?date=2026-02-17&category=reddit#item-137c5034f2ce) coverage from 2026-02-17, Levelsio's major thread: He connected Claude Code to Telegram to chat directly with production sites. Describes security incident where someone social-engineered OpenClaw to modify server. Now uses claude-code-telegram locked to his messages only. Demonstrates live bug fixing and feature building via chat.
items.reasoning: Extremely high engagement (2858 likes, 504K views). Rich technical content from a high-profile indie maker. Covers: (1) practical production AI agent deployment, (2) real security incident with social engineering of AI agents, (3) comparison of OpenClaw vs custom Telegram integration, (4) live demo of conversational development. Important signal about how developers are integrating AI coding tools into production workflows.

items.title: For those who want to understand, this chart shows how well Claude Sonnet models are able to use a w...
items.content:
For those who want to understand, this chart shows how well Claude Sonnet models are able to use a web browser.

Anthropic says “Sonnet 4.6 users are seeing human-level capability in tasks like navigating a complex spreadsheet or filling out a multi-step web form, before pulling it all together across multiple browser tabs.

The model certainly still lags behind the most skilled humans at using computers. But the rate of progress is remarkable nonetheless. It means that computer use is much more useful for a range of work tasks—and that substantially more capable models are within reach.”

Spend a minute thinking about what this means.
items.author: @mattshumer_
items.summary: Continuing our coverage from yesterday's [Social](/?date=2026-02-18&category=social#item-de5b749a12c8), Matt Shumer shares detailed analysis of Claude Sonnet 4.6's computer use capabilities, citing Anthropic's claim of 'human-level capability' for tasks like navigating spreadsheets and multi-step web forms. Notes the model still lags behind most skilled humans but rate of progress is remarkable.
items.reasoning: Highest importance post in batch. Detailed analysis of Claude Sonnet 4.6's computer use capabilities from a credible source. This directly relates to yesterday's Sonnet 4.6 release (GA: 2026-02-17). The claim of approaching human-level computer use is a significant milestone. Strong engagement (25K views, 137 likes).

items.title: Most Claude Code turns are short (median ~45 seconds). But the longest turns show where autonomy is ...
items.content:
Most Claude Code turns are short (median ~45 seconds). But the longest turns show where autonomy is heading.

In three months, the 99.9th percentile turn duration nearly doubled, from under 25 minutes to over 45 minutes. This growth is smooth across model releases. https://t.co/cF0a4Td1si
items.author: @AnthropicAI

items.reasoning: Strong quantitative evidence of increasing agent autonomy duration. The smoothness across model releases suggests user behavior drives this, not just model capability. High engagement (54K views).

If you’re building a business on top of the Agent SDK, you should use an API key instead. We’ll make sure that’s clearer in our docs.

items.title: Today we introduced Lyria 3, @GoogleDeepMind's latest music generation model, which you can use to t...
items.content:
Today we introduced Lyria 3, @GoogleDeepMind's latest music generation model, which you can use to turn your ideas into musical tracks through prompts or even photos. Here are 3 tips (and fun facts!) to help you make the most out today's launch:

1. We intentionally built this model to have extremely high customization character limits, so you can feel the full freedom of flexibility. From a pithy haiku to an emotional short story, Lyria 3 allows you to lose yourself in the details.

2. Get creative! When designing this model, we wanted to cater a broad and diverse range of musical tastes (not just pop music). Whether it's unique combinations of instruments or blends of seemingly clashing genres, feel free to experiment.

3. When creating a track, you can prompt the style of music and Gemini can suggest lyrics that fit your theme. Or, you could request specific lyrics transforming the most mundane messages into your very own musical. For example, when testing the model, the team found success in creating tracks with critically acclaimed lyrics like "Check your email" or "Please RSVP to my event.”

Lyria 3 is rolling out today in beta in the @GeminiApp for 18+ users. Have fun!
items.author: @GoogleAI
items.summary: Google AI details Lyria 3 features: high customization character limits, diverse genre support, lyrics generation, and creative prompting tips including using photos as input.
items.reasoning: Detailed product feature announcement with very high engagement (212K views). Provides key details about Lyria 3 capabilities.
items.summary_html: Google AI details Lyria 3 features: high customization character limits, diverse genre support, lyrics generation, and creative prompting tips including using photos as input.
items.content_html:
Today we introduced Lyria 3, @GoogleDeepMind's latest music generation model, which you can use to turn your ideas into musical tracks through prompts or even photos. Here are 3 tips (and fun facts!) to help you make the most out today's launch:

1. We intentionally built this model to have extremely high customization character limits, so you can feel the full freedom of flexibility. From a pithy haiku to an emotional short story, Lyria 3 allows you to lose yourself in the details.

2. Get creative! When designing this model, we wanted to cater a broad and diverse range of musical tastes (not just pop music). Whether it's unique combinations of instruments or blends of seemingly clashing genres, feel free to experiment.

3. When creating a track, you can prompt the style of music and Gemini can suggest lyrics that fit your theme. Or, you could request specific lyrics transforming the most mundane messages into your very own musical. For example, when testing the model, the team found success in creating tracks with critically acclaimed lyrics like "Check your email" or "Please RSVP to my event.”

Lyria 3 is rolling out today in beta in the @GeminiApp for 18+ users. Have fun!

items.title: I once needed money, so I joined an offshore software outsourcing company in Ukraine as a shit-coder...
items.content:
I once needed money, so I joined an offshore software outsourcing company in Ukraine as a shit-coder for $100/month.

I had just finished university and had no experience as a coder. In my first week at work, they gave me a bug to fix. The application was https://t.co/0soRTHa58g (it's funny that the application still exists and looks as shitty as I remember it 25 years ago).

The application had a huge codebase in ColdFusion; it was written in India by folks who asked slightly more than $100/month for their work, and it looked and worked accordingly.

As I said, I had zero experience with debugging such a large application, so I asked my manager's manager for help.

He looked at the error message that mentioned the file where it failed, then went to the directory with this file, searched for the variable that caused the issue, then saw in what file this variable was mentioned. Then he mounted like that about three levels, found the file where it was created and what values it could contain at the creation time, compared the observed value and the expected one, and figured out what was wrong at the time the variable was created.

I was so impressed that one could start in some unknown place and, by doing variable tracing to the source through files using simple string match, come to the place where it all becomes clear.

With time, I realized that this is a pattern that a software developer uses most of the time when debugging: string search -> file search -> string search -> file search.

This is why good developers keep their codebase neat: this simplifies the manual search and understanding of what to search for next.

The LLMs have learned this simple but powerful pattern the moment they were given access to grep and glob with one difference: they don't need the code to look neat; they don't analyze the text the same way humans do. So, for an LLM, it doesn't matter whether you, a human, think that its code is maintainable *by you* or not. It's maintainable by using the attention-glob-grep combo, and that's enough.

Saying that you don't accept AI-generated code because you cannot maintain it by hand is the same as saying that you don’t accept mass-produced electronics because you can’t manually fix or replace every component.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov shares a story about learning debugging in Ukraine, argues LLMs learned the grep-glob pattern of debugging and don't need human-readable code. Claims rejecting AI code because humans can't maintain it is like rejecting mass-produced electronics because you can't fix components.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful, well-articulated argument about AI-generated code paradigm. Good engagement (36K views). Provocative thesis about maintainability shifting from human to AI.

Claims rejecting AI code because humans can't maintain it is like rejecting mass-produced electronics because you can't fix components.

The application had a huge codebase in ColdFusion; it was written in India by folks who asked slightly more than $100/month for their work, and it looked and worked accordingly.

As I said, I had zero experience with debugging such a large application, so I asked my manager's manager for help.

He looked at the error message that mentioned the file where it failed, then went to the directory with this file, searched for the variable that caused the issue, then saw in what file this variable was mentioned. Then he mounted like that about three levels, found the file where it was created and what values it could contain at the creation time, compared the observed value and the expected one, and figured out what was wrong at the time the variable was created.

I was so impressed that one could start in some unknown place and, by doing variable tracing to the source through files using simple string match, come to the place where it all becomes clear.

With time, I realized that this is a pattern that a software developer uses most of the time when debugging: string search -> file search -> string search -> file search.

This is why good developers keep their codebase neat: this simplifies the manual search and understanding of what to search for next.

The LLMs have learned this simple but powerful pattern the moment they were given access to grep and glob with one difference: they don't need the code to look neat; they don't analyze the text the same way humans do. So, for an LLM, it doesn't matter whether you, a human, think that its code is maintainable *by you* or not. It's maintainable by using the attention-glob-grep combo, and that's enough.

Saying that you don't accept AI-generated code because you cannot maintain it by hand is the same as saying that you don’t accept mass-produced electronics because you can’t manually fix or replace every component.

items.title: I don't understand how anyone can be impressed by this. Okay, wow, a machine flickered some photogra...
items.content: I don't understand how anyone can be impressed by this. Okay, wow, a machine flickered some photographs together fast enough to trick your eye into seeing movement. Okay, splendid. The fundamental problem with these moving pictures — and why I will never be interested in a cinematograph show, no matter how clever the illusions become — is that I simply have no interest in the stories a machine tells. I don't want to watch a projector's story any more than I want to sit and listen to a phonograph tell me about its hopes and dreams. If the story has no soul behind it, no living actor before you breathing the words, no stage, no real theater — then it lacks the one and only thing that can make it worthwhile or interesting.

items.title: I have not seen even a *glimpse* of any kind of understanding from any of the AI models that we curr...
items.content: I have not seen even a *glimpse* of any kind of understanding from any of the AI models that we currently have. That's not saying that they are not useful - most of them are extremely useful - but so far I pretty much have to provide all the context and big picture thinking when approaching any project in collaboration with AI. It's like having a very capable autistic idiot savant as your assistant.

items.title: Attention in transformers works by having each token look at every other token in the context — whic...
items.content:
Attention in transformers works by having each token look at every other token in the context — which gets very expensive as contexts grow longer, and modern use cases like document search or step-by-step reasoning often involve tens or hundreds of thousands of tokens.

This paper from Microsoft Research starts from two observations:

1. After the softmax operation, most of those attention weights are already near zero anyway (only a small fraction of tokens actually matter for any given computation);
2. Which tokens matter tends to stay consistent across nearby layers of the network.

From these two facts, the authors build a method that does the full, expensive attention calculation only in a few selected layers, then reuses those results in the layers in between — skipping most of the work without asking the model to retrain or change its weights at all. 

An important feature of the proposed approach is the engineering around it: how to automatically pick which layers to treat as reference points for any given model, how to handle the fact that different attention heads within the same layer may care about different tokens, and how to make this run efficiently on actual hardware. 

The reported speedups — around 4× for the generation phase (on top of what GQA already provides; see this paper to learn about GQA https://t.co/p9wUJlsGvx) — come with benchmarks showing the outputs remain close to what a full attention model would produce, including on hard math reasoning problems where earlier methods of this kind had struggled.

Read with AI tutor: https://t.co/PP7mpDiH2n

Download the PDF: https://t.co/hQjP0lO3kM
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov explains a Microsoft Research paper on efficient attention: most attention weights are near-zero after softmax and important tokens stay consistent across layers, enabling 4x speedup by computing full attention only in selected layers.
items.reasoning: Clear technical explanation of an important efficiency paper. Attention optimization is highly relevant for scaling. Good educational content.
items.summary_html: Burkov explains a Microsoft Research paper on efficient attention: most attention weights are near-zero after softmax and important tokens stay consistent across layers, enabling 4x speedup by computing full attention only in selected layers.

This paper from Microsoft Research starts from two observations:

2. Which tokens matter tends to stay consistent across nearby layers of the network.

From these two facts, the authors build a method that does the full, expensive attention calculation only in a few selected layers, then reuses those results in the layers in between — skipping most of the work without asking the model to retrain or change its weights at all.

An important feature of the proposed approach is the engineering around it: how to automatically pick which layers to treat as reference points for any given model, how to handle the fact that different attention heads within the same layer may care about different tokens, and how to make this run efficiently on actual hardware.

The reported speedups — around 4× for the generation phase (on top of what GQA already provides; see this paper to learn about GQA https://t.co/p9wUJlsGvx) — come with benchmarks showing the outputs remain close to what a full attention model would produce, including on hard math reasoning problems where earlier methods of this kind had struggled.

items.title:
Whoever thinks that LLMs write reliable code should be kept away from computers.

Because of how LLM...
items.content:
Whoever thinks that LLMs write reliable code should be kept away from computers.

Because of how LLMs are trained, they write code that works most of the time.

There's nothing in the training objective about efficiency or reliability.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov warns that LLMs write code that works 'most of the time' but not reliably or efficiently, due to training objectives. Says people who think otherwise should be 'kept away from computers.'
items.reasoning: Important counterpoint to AI coding hype from a respected ML author. Good engagement (35K views). Interesting paired with his other pro-AI-code posts.

items.title: @nicobaogim I already posted about this. If an AI-generated module of your software cannot be mainta...
items.content:
@nicobaogim I already posted about this. If an AI-generated module of your software cannot be maintained by the AI, you  ask the AI to write new pice of code using the spec and the tests. So, you maintain the spec and the tests, and that's enough.

Yes, it will require additional testing once de regenerated from scratch, and if it's a user-facing module, some users will need to adapt to a slightly different look and feel, but that's the same drawback we accept when we purchase a non-repairable kettle and need to get used to a new kettle when the previous one is broken.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov argues AI-generated code that can't be maintained by humans is fine - if AI can't maintain a module, you regenerate it from spec and tests. Compares to non-repairable electronics.
items.reasoning: Interesting and somewhat provocative argument about the maintainability paradigm shift with AI-generated code. Low engagement for a reply but thoughtful.
items.summary_html: Burkov argues AI-generated code that can't be maintained by humans is fine - if AI can't maintain a module, you regenerate it from spec and tests. Compares to non-repairable electronics.
items.content_html:
@nicobaogim I already posted about this. If an AI-generated module of your software cannot be maintained by the AI, you ask the AI to write new pice of code using the spec and the tests. So, you maintain the spec and the tests, and that's enough.

Yes, it will require additional testing once de regenerated from scratch, and if it's a user-facing module, some users will need to adapt to a slightly different look and feel, but that's the same drawback we accept when we purchase a non-repairable kettle and need to get used to a new kettle when the previous one is broken.

items.title: I have a friend who barely finished high school and just called me with a few questions about vibe c...
items.content:
I have a friend who barely finished high school and just called me with a few questions about vibe coding.

People aren't talking enough about this:

Non-developers are building real businesses with AI.

It's not just landing pages and demos, but real products that are helping them make money.

This friend is building a simple app for dentists to place orders for implants. He marks them up and sources them from China.

Software was a bottleneck, but that's changing.

I'm currently collaborating with Emergent. They doubled from $50M to $100M ARR in a single month because people are desperate to build software.

6M+ builders across 190+ countries have shipped over 7M apps on the platform.

This is what's happening with AI.

And now, Emergent is removing another barrier:

You can now build software from your mobile phone, including web, iOS, and Android apps. Click a button and publish them directly to the App Store and Play Store.

The cost of trying new things is now close to zero.

The cost of going from an idea to a working product is also decreasing by the day.

By the way, my friend doesn't care about code quality, architecture, or best practices. I tried my best, but he doesn't want to listen.

He cares about selling more product.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: svpino discusses how non-developers are building real businesses with AI/vibe coding, citing a friend building a dental implant ordering app. Promotes Emergent platform (doubled from $50M to $100M ARR in one month, 6M+ builders, 7M apps) and their new mobile app building capability.

• Chat/non-coding: ...
items.content:
Rough heuristic on effort + thinking:

• Quick tasks: effort = low, thinking off
• Chat/non-coding: effort = low, extended thinking w/ 16k budget
• Coding/agentic: effort = medium, extended thinking w/ 16k budget
• Autonomous agents, comp use: effort = high, adaptive thinking

Rough heuristic on effort + thinking:

• Quick tasks: effort = low, thinking off

• Chat/non-coding: effort = low, extended thinking w/ 16k budget

• Coding/agentic: effort = medium, extended thinking w/ 16k budget

• Autonomous agents, comp use: effort = high, adaptive thinking

items.content:
We're looking for founders building agentic apps.

• This is the TinyFish Accelerator.
• 9 weeks.
• $2M seed pool.

We want you to build agents that navigate real web interfaces and execute real transactions.

We don't want wrappers. We want agents from the ground up.

To apply, you need a working app + record a demo, and publish it on X.

We have 15+ partner companies across the full stack: Google for Startups, v0 by Vercel, ElevenLabs, Fireworks, MongoDB, Composio, AgentOps, Dify, and more.

They all will provide credits, API keys, and direct engineering support to every founder in the cohort.

What you get:

• Free TinyFish API keys
• Engineering support and product feedback from the team
• Free credits + direct engineering access from partner companies
• Weekly business mentorship sessions with founders and leaders
• Off-the-record community meetups
• Pitch directly to Robin Vasan (Mango Capital)

The applications are open starting today. Rolling admissions through March 29.

Apply → https://t.co/xON0UCoSTl

items.title:
The new type of software is going to be “dynamic”:

- A strong foundation of data access layer and i...
items.content:
The new type of software is going to be “dynamic”:

- A strong foundation of data access layer and integrations
- A smart LLM layer to operate on the data
- On-demand dynamic UI for the end users, most other interactions become conversational.

For example, an email app will work like this: I connect with my email account, then I just talk (or chat) to the app.

If I want to check emails the old way, I would just say “show me an email review interface, group incoming emails by sender and date”, then the AI will show me an on demand UI so I can scroll and click around.

If I use any particular UI a lot, I will just bookmark that UI somewhere and later I just reopen it directly.

All of these UI will become hyper-personalized that fit with the habit and workflows of each user individually. There is no need to design a UI for your software anymore. The same email app may look completely different for each person.
items.author: @tdinh_me
items.summary: tdinh_me envisions future software as 'dynamic': strong data layer + LLM operations + on-demand personalized UI generated conversationally. Uses email app as example where each user's interface is uniquely generated.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful vision post about the future of software UI/UX with AI. 113 likes, good engagement. The dynamic UI concept is novel and well-articulated.
items.summary_html: tdinh_me envisions future software as 'dynamic': strong data layer + LLM operations + on-demand personalized UI generated conversationally. Uses email app as example where each user's interface is uniquely generated.
items.content_html:
The new type of software is going to be “dynamic”:

  • A strong foundation of data access layer and integrations
  • A smart LLM layer to operate on the data
  • On-demand dynamic UI for the end users, most other interactions become conversational.

For example, an email app will work like this: I connect with my email account, then I just talk (or chat) to the app.

If I want to check emails the old way, I would just say “show me an email review interface, group incoming emails by sender and date”, then the AI will show me an on demand UI so I can scroll and click around.

If I use any particular UI a lot, I will just bookmark that UI somewhere and later I just reopen it directly.

All of these UI will become hyper-personalized that fit with the habit and workflows of each user individually. There is no need to design a UI for your software anymore. The same email app may look completely different for each person.

It's worth noting that prompti...

items.summary_html: Anthropic employee discusses .cursorrules equivalent for Claude Code: mentions CLAUDE.md as the configuration file, notes Claude prompting differs from other models, and describes how to reference shared config files via @ mentions.
items.content_html:
@ryancarson Will bring up https://t.co/NbSB8oRXTM to the team again.

It's worth noting that prompting Claude is different than other models and so it's important for users to opt-in to this. Sharing prompts between all models reduces performance a bit, but totally understand the convenience of it.

The easiest way to do this right now is to @ mention https://t.co/NbSB8oRXTM in your https://t.co/v7BCo2FhNH which allows you to opt-in to including https://t.co/NbSB8oRXTM and then have separate instructions for Claude as well.

There's a lot of new people making more interesting things with AI. Just a reminder that if you are making films with AI and experimenting with new technologies for storytelling, there is an annual festival that celebrates exactly that.

Runway's fourth annual AI Festival is hosted at the Lincoln Center in NYC and at the Broad Stage in Los Angeles.

To give you a sense of scale, we went from 300 submissions in the first edition in 2023 to 6,000 submissions in 2026. 

Now is the time.

items.title:
Assume it will go wrong.

Work back from there.

Israeli thinking an entrepreneur building a compute...
items.content:
Assume it will go wrong.

Work back from there.

Israeli thinking an entrepreneur building a computer vision company once told me in Tel Aviv.

I have the most complete lists of those working on AI anywhere. Really of smart people in all fields.

Lucky for us the smartest humans alive are on the AI team.

If they can’t save us we weren’t meant to be saved.

I am not worried.

By the way I shared the doomsday scenario before anyone I remember seeing here on X. 

The AI I am using from @blevlabs is quite amazing. It is changing how I think about building.

It is way smarter than me at reading X. Something I have done every day for 19 years. And wrote a book about social media before this started.

We aren’t done yet.

items.title: The number of people mindlessly filming multi-hour “walking through the city” videos would astound y...
items.content:
The number of people mindlessly filming multi-hour “walking through the city” videos would astound you. 

They’re mostly recording on their phone and not a DLSR. They’re almost always holding a tripod stick or a gimbal. Vast majority film horizontally. A select few narrate over it. 

Saw it in Sydney. 

Saw it in Vegas. 

See it much more in NYC. 

My face is in at least 20 of them. 

Do people watch these? Is it a revenue grab on YouTube? Is it training data? This is an instance where I wish more people had meta raybans.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Allie K Miller observes the trend of people filming multi-hour walking-through-city videos, speculating whether it's for YouTube revenue or training data, and wishing more people had Meta Ray-Bans
items.reasoning: Interesting cultural observation from AI leader about the intersection of content creation, training data generation, and wearable tech (Meta Ray-Bans). Good engagement (5K views). Raises relevant question about whether street filming is for AI training data.
items.summary_html: Allie K Miller observes the trend of people filming multi-hour walking-through-city videos, speculating whether it's for YouTube revenue or training data, and wishing more people had Meta Ray-Bans

Saw it in Sydney.

My face is in at least 20 of them.

Do people watch these? Is it a revenue grab on YouTube? Is it training data? This is an instance where I wish more people had meta raybans.

items.content:
Just for fun: AI music video contest

In celebration of Claude Code’s 1 yr bday, I will give $1000 USD to the best* music video for “Ode to Claude Code”

To submit:
- Find the song on Spotify
- Use AI to create music video
- Reply here by Feb 21, noon PT
- Extra credit for sharing tech stack
- Extra extra credit for having and agent do everything

*By best, I mean my favorite since I’m judging

items.content:
@DaveDunkin1 First of all there are hundreds of videos where it reacts far faster than you can and avoids the wreck.

Second of all electric cars have all their weight underneath them.

They don’t flip.

Third of all they are far far safer in a head on crash than a gas vehicle.

Fourth of all if in a crash they stop and pull over if they can.

items.content: @toms_dome @layers no it definitely does not need that level of detail. but the point here is that when generating a new feature, you are already explaining it in natural language. there is small but real friction in having to type it again for marketing purposes. code aware marketing agent removes that friction.

items.title:
The shift is this:

UGC is turning into a programmable AI system.

Instead of “hire a creator,” it b...
items.content:
The shift is this:

UGC is turning into a programmable AI system.

Instead of “hire a creator,” it becomes:

→ pick an avatar + voice
→ upload a script
→ add product visuals
→ generate influencer-style videos in minutes

That’s not content creation.
That’s content manufacturing.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon describes UGC becoming a 'programmable AI system' where avatar, voice, script, and product visuals combine to generate influencer-style videos in minutes
items.reasoning: Describes a meaningful shift in content creation toward AI-generated UGC, though promotional in nature
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon describes UGC becoming a 'programmable AI system' where avatar, voice, script, and product visuals combine to generate influencer-style videos in minutes
items.content_html:
The shift is this:

UGC is turning into a programmable AI system.

Instead of “hire a creator,” it becomes:

→ pick an avatar + voice

→ upload a script

→ add product visuals

→ generate influencer-style videos in minutes

That’s not content creation.

That’s content manufacturing.

items.title:
And the real advantage isn’t realism.

It’s speed.

When you can generate unlimited variations, you ...
items.content:
And the real advantage isn’t realism.

It’s speed.

When you can generate unlimited variations, you can:

→ A/B test hooks daily
→ iterate messaging fast
→ deploy across TikTok, Meta, Instagram, YouTube
→ stop guessing what converts

This is performance marketing getting its factory line.

@bdambrosio The most expensive part of ChapterPal (from a cost-of-running perspective) is OCR, so the pricing model is built around it. The cheapest plan, at $10/month, gives you basic OCR quality and a few OCR pages, while more expensive plans offer better quality OCR and more pages.

I'll soon make available a direct LaTeX to markdown conversion, which will reduce the platform's dependency on OCR and the prices should go down.

items.reasoning: Product-specific detail about a personal project. Very low engagement.

I'll soon make available a direct LaTeX to markdown conversion, which will reduce the platform's dependency on OCR and the prices should go down.

items.title:
UGC works, but it has a scaling problem:

→ Creators are expensive
→ Filming takes weeks
→ Testing i...
items.content:
UGC works, but it has a scaling problem:

→ Creators are expensive
→ Filming takes weeks
→ Testing is slow
→ You get 3 versions when you need 300

The bottleneck isn’t creativity.

It’s production throughput.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon outlines UGC's scaling problem: expensive creators, slow filming, limited testing, insufficient variations
items.reasoning: Sets up problem statement for AI UGC solution, part of promotional thread
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon outlines UGC's scaling problem: expensive creators, slow filming, limited testing, insufficient variations
items.content_html:
UGC works, but it has a scaling problem:

→ Creators are expensive

→ Filming takes weeks

→ Testing is slow

→ You get 3 versions when you need 300

The bottleneck isn’t creativity.

It’s production throughput.

items.summary: Levelsio discusses Chinese stock market investments and ETF fees.

items.summary_html: Levelsio discusses Chinese stock market investments and ETF fees.
items.content_html:
@marclou @daniel_nguyenx I hold a lot of ICHN for years, 0.28% fee vs MSCI's China ETF 0.59% fee (above limit that shd be 0.50% imho)

Problem I learnt with Chinese stock market that it doesn't matter if China gets rich, you won't notice it in the prices of stocks there as it's not a transparent market like the US

So not sure what to do now
